summary: Auth/RBAC posture is mature (Bearer-token gating on most service-role routes, anti-enumeration on forgot-password, rate-limited public endpoints). Highest-risk surfaces are: an unauthenticated Drive stream proxy that exposes any drive fileId as bytes, two service-role routes (proxy-upload, drive/finalize) with zero auth, an unverified reset-password flow that trusts URL access_tokens, and an open password-confirm redirect chain that emits tokens in URL parameters consumable from browser history/referer.

findings.title: drive/proxy-upload has zero authentication — anyone can write to the team Drive
findings.impact: Any unauthenticated internet visitor can POST a multipart form-data file (up to Vercel body cap) and have the server upload it to Ten80Ten's Google Shared Drive using the service-account token. They can also call setPublicPermission via the same path, then read it back via /api/drive/stream. This abuses Drive storage quota, lets attackers host arbitrary content (illegal material, malware, phishing pages) from a legitimate Ten80Ten-controlled URL, and bypasses any team RBAC. The sibling /api/drive/upload route IS authenticated (Bearer + rate-limit); this one is silently public.
findings.repro: curl -X POST https://smm.ten80ten.com/api/drive/proxy-upload -F 'file=@evil.html' -F 'folder=media-library' -F 'fileName=evil.html' -F 'mimeType=text/html'  # returns { fileId, url: '/api/drive/stream?id=...' } without any 401
findings.fix: Add the same `requireBearerUser` (or `requireBearerTeamRole`) gate that /api/drive/upload uses at the top of the handler (lines 22-40 of /drive/upload). Also add per-user rate limiting via consume('drive-proxy-upload', ...) and reject mimeTypes outside an image/video allowlist.

findings.title: drive/finalize has zero authentication — anyone can mark any Drive file public
findings.impact: Caller passes any Drive file id the service account can see, and the route flips it to public (anyone-with-link can read) using setPublicPermission. Combined with the fact that the service account has Content Manager access to the Shared Drive (per the upload error message), an attacker can enumerate file ids (via realtime channel echoes or audit_log scraping) and publicize internal-only files. fileId is also not validated for shape (no UUID/regex check), so the value is passed directly to the Google Drive API.
findings.repro: curl -X POST https://smm.ten80ten.com/api/drive/finalize -H 'content-type: application/json' -d '{"fileId":"<any-known-drive-id>"}'  # returns 200 with stream URL + metadata; permission is now world-readable
findings.fix: Add requireBearerTeamRole(req, AI_WRITER_ROLES) at the top of POST. Optionally also verify the fileId is one that this user/workspace actually owns by joining media_assets on workspace_id. Validate fileId shape with /^[\w-]+$/.

findings.title: drive/stream proxy intentionally skips auth — any caller with a fileId pulls bytes
findings.impact: CORS narrowing is a browser-only control — curl / Python / server-side scrapers ignore Access-Control-Allow-Origin. Anyone who learns a Drive fileId (leaked in realtime payloads, audit_log_v2 metadata, error messages, or via SEC-002) can pull the file bytes anonymously. The route comment itself flags this; the follow-up signed-URL plan is unmerged. Drive ids are 33-char base64; not brute-forceable on their own, but they leak through the URL/email/asset_urls JSON columns and are persisted client-side. The 'private, max-age=86400, immutable' cache header makes the bytes additionally cacheable on intermediaries that respect Vary.
findings.repro: curl 'https://smm.ten80ten.com/api/drive/stream?id=<known-id>' -o leaked.png  # no Origin header, no auth, returns the bytes
findings.fix: Issue short-lived HMAC-signed URLs (token = HMAC(fileId|userId|exp, SIGNING_SECRET), embed in the URL as ?token=, validate before fetching from Drive). The follow-up plan is already referenced in the file (lines 47-49). For a stop-gap, require a Supabase access-token cookie and skip auth only for same-origin Referer.

findings.title: OAuth/Recovery tokens leaked through URL parameters into browser history & Referer
findings.impact: Both Supabase access_token AND refresh_token are placed in the URL query string of the redirect target. Consequences: (1) browser history persists them; anyone who later borrows the laptop sees them, (2) any third-party Google Fonts / analytics request fired from /auth/setup or /auth/reset-password will send the full URL as Referer, leaking tokens to those vendors, (3) the tokens land in any browser extension/proxy/server log that records URLs. Refresh tokens specifically have a long TTL (default 7 days) so the leak window is large. The downstream pages then call setSession(accessToken, refreshToken) which establishes a full session for whoever holds the URL.
findings.repro: Open the password-reset email link; check browser history → you'll see the full access_token + refresh_token. Or check Network tab — any request from the setup/reset page (Google Fonts loader call to fonts.googleapis.com is loaded from Inter font import in layout.tsx) carries Referer with the tokens.
findings.fix: After verifyOtp succeeds, set HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite=Lax cookies named `sb-access-token` and `sb-refresh-token` on the redirect response and redirect to /auth/setup or /auth/reset-password WITHOUT the tokens in the URL. The downstream client pages should fetch the session via supabase.auth.getSession() which reads from the cookies via the Supabase SDK. Alternatively, use a fragment (#access_token=...) instead of a query string — fragments don't get sent in Referer or stored in server logs.

findings.title: Confirm route allows `signup` and `magiclink` types as redirect handlers — open redirect surface + privilege grant
findings.impact: Supabase auth allows admin-created users to be confirmed via the `signup` and `magiclink` types. The application's invite flow ALREADY goes through type=invite for a reason — it routes to /auth/setup which enforces password+profile completion AND then signs the user out so they must re-login. signup/magiclink paths bypass /auth/setup entirely (fall through to '/' on line 67). If anyone calls generateLink with type=signup or type=magiclink for an existing-but-pending-only team_member, the user gets dropped straight into the dashboard with the still-default tempPassword set by admin.auth.admin.createUser. Combined with the orphaned admin.auth.admin.createUser tempPassword = 'crypto.randomUUID() + "!Aa1"' the user can never re-login normally without going through /auth/setup. Worse: an attacker who steals an invite token_hash can swap ?type=invite for ?type=magiclink (same hash works) and bypass the password-set step entirely, getting a session bound to the invitee's email.
findings.repro: Intercept an invite email. Change `&type=invite` to `&type=magiclink` in the confirm URL → user is redirected straight to the dashboard with a valid session and no password set. The team_members row remains in `status='pending'`, but the auth-context only checks supabase.auth.getUser(), not the team_members.status, on most routes.
findings.fix: Tighten VALID_TYPES to ['invite', 'recovery'] only. Reject all other types with a 400. The team's actual auth flows only use those two types (verified by reading invite/forgot-password routes); the broader list was speculative.

findings.title: workspace/provision auto-provisions ANY authenticated user not on team_members as 'editor' (pending)
findings.impact: If an attacker can create an arbitrary Supabase auth user (which is possible if Supabase signup is enabled on the project, or via leaked invite/magic links per SEC-005) for an email NOT in team_members, this endpoint inserts a workspace_members row with role='editor' status='pending'. While 'pending' members are blocked by is_active_workspace_member RLS for posts, the row's mere existence pollutes the membership list and the 'pending' status check is rarely re-evaluated elsewhere. Some downstream routes (drive/upload, publish-jobs) check workspace_members.role without filtering on status='active' — verify each one. The fallback role of 'editor' is also chosen silently rather than safest-by-default (which would be 'viewer' or nothing).
findings.repro: POST /api/workspace/provision with Authorization: Bearer <token-of-an-auth.users-row-not-in-team_members>  → 200 + provisioned=true + workspace_id. Now query SELECT role FROM workspace_members WHERE user_id = <that-id> and see role='editor' status='pending'.
findings.fix: Only provision if a team_members row exists. Drop the `|| "editor"` default — if tm is null, return 403. Restrict role to the role declared in team_members exactly; reject mismatched email casing instead of using ilike (or normalize before insert).

findings.title: auto-revise webhook has no replay or timestamp protection — replayed payloads enqueue duplicate revise jobs
findings.impact: Two issues: (1) The secret comparison uses === which is NOT timing-safe, leaking byte-by-byte info to an attacker who can measure response latency (mitigated somewhat by network jitter on Vercel). (2) The webhook accepts replays — there is no nonce, no timestamp window, no signature over body. Anyone who once observes a valid Authorization header (TLS termination at a misconfigured proxy, server logs, debug output) can replay an old payload to re-enqueue revise jobs forever, burning OpenAI budget and creating revision storms. The route does check `inflight` jobs per post, but the attacker can change postId/workspace_id in the replayed body to bypass that check.
findings.repro: Capture the SUPABASE_WEBHOOK_SECRET (e.g., from a debug log entry). POST to /api/ai/auto-revise/webhook with a crafted body { type: 'UPDATE', table: 'posts', old_record:{stage:'idea'}, record:{stage:'revision_needed', id:'<known-post>', workspace_id:'<id>', generated_by_model:'gpt-image-2', notes:'Fix submitted — please redo this'} } and the captured Bearer. The handler enqueues a revise job, consumes OpenAI credit, and posts a revised version even though no human triggered it.
findings.fix: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual after asserting equal length. Switch to Supabase's HMAC webhook signing (sign over body + timestamp; reject timestamps older than 5min). Re-validate that payload.record.workspace_id matches a known workspace and the post-id exists with the claimed workspace.

findings.title: ai/auto-revise/process also uses non-timing-safe secret compare
findings.evidence:
if (bearer && cronSecret && bearer === cronSecret) return true;
if (trigger && triggerSecret && trigger === triggerSecret) return true;
findings.impact: Same class of timing-side-channel as SEC-007. The CRON_SECRET protects the queue drainer which calls OpenAI for every queued job; an attacker who guesses or recovers the secret can DoS the OpenAI budget by triggering /api/ai/auto-revise/process repeatedly, draining queued jobs in a hot loop. Vercel cron also leaks the secret via /api/admin endpoint logs if any developer dumps headers.
findings.repro: Same attack pattern as SEC-007 once the secret is known — POST to /api/ai/auto-revise/process with x-trigger-secret: <stolen> from any source. Each call drains up to 3 queued jobs.
findings.fix: Replace `bearer === cronSecret` with crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(bearer), Buffer.from(cronSecret)) after length check.

findings.title: Setup page trusts JWT user_metadata for invite_role without re-checking team_members
findings.impact: The setup page renders the invited role/email from JWT user_metadata without re-validating it against the server. The team_members row UPDATE at line 124 also uses `user.email` (from getUser) so this client-side display alone isn't a privilege escalation BUT: (a) it sets a false expectation of role for the invitee, (b) the team_members.update at lines 125-133 fires without checking the auth user actually corresponds to the team_members row (relies on RLS but RLS on team_members likely permits self-update by email match). An admin who flipped the invitee's role between invite and setup will see the OLD role in the user_metadata-embedded JWT; the team_members row may show a different role, causing role-drift and confusion.
findings.repro: Admin invites Alice as editor → email sent. Admin then changes Alice's role to viewer in the dashboard. Alice clicks the invite link → setup page shows 'Invited as editor' (stale). Alice completes setup, status flips to active, but team_members.role may be reverted by the client-side trust path if the schema permits it.
findings.fix: Drop the JWT-metadata decode block. Fetch the canonical team_members row via supabase.from('team_members').select('name,role,email').eq('email', user.email).single() after setSession, and display from THAT. Validate role server-side before any update.

findings.title: admin/backfill-media iterates ALL posts globally with service-role, no workspace filter
findings.impact: Selects EVERY post and EVERY media_asset across the entire database (not scoped to the caller's workspace_id) using the service-role client. For a single-tenant baseline workspace this is currently moot, but the moment multi-tenant lands (the AGENTS.md notes a 2026-05-20 multi-tenant commitment with a 6-sprint playbook) this route becomes a cross-tenant data leak: any admin (role superadmin/admin/owner of workspace A) can run backfill and inadvertently insert media_assets cross-workspace, since post.workspace_id is preserved on insert but used_in arrays span all workspaces. The auth check correctly verifies ADMIN_ROLES on team_members but does NOT scope to the admin's workspace.
findings.repro: Once multi-tenant lands, admin of workspace A POSTs to /api/admin/backfill-media; server inserts media_assets rows into workspace_id values of posts belonging to OTHER tenants. No per-workspace check exists.
findings.fix: Before SELECTing posts, resolve the actor's workspace_id from workspace_members and filter the posts query by `.eq('workspace_id', actorWorkspaceId)`. Same for media_assets. This is a multi-tenant pre-req.

findings.title: presence/departure beacon validates token but accepts ANY token shape, no rate-limit, lets attackers refresh presence indefinitely
findings.impact: Beacon endpoint takes a Supabase access token in body and upserts user_presence using service role. Two issues: (1) no rate-limit — an attacker with a stolen token can spam this endpoint to keep a user marked 'online' indefinitely, evading the idle/offline UI. (2) Always returns 204 regardless of token validity, so the endpoint is also a token-validation oracle (the upsert error is logged but never surfaced) — but more importantly, no rate-limit means it doubles as a free way to verify hundreds of stolen tokens per second by side-channel (response time changes when admin.auth.getUser fails).
findings.repro: navigator.sendBeacon('/api/presence/departure', JSON.stringify({token: stolenToken})) in a tight loop. user_presence.last_seen_at updates every call. Latency observable between valid-token vs invalid-token responses leaks token validity.
findings.fix: Add IP-based rate-limit (e.g., consume('presence-departure:ip', ip, 60, 60)) and per-token rate-limit. Compare response time across the two paths; if measurable, return after a fixed delay using setTimeout to mask.

findings.title: notifications/* routes (mention, approved, awaiting-approval, revision) have NO authentication
findings.impact: All four notification routes (notifications/mention, notifications/approved, notifications/awaiting-approval, notifications/revision) only rate-limit by IP and require zero auth. Any unauthenticated visitor can POST a forged body and trigger Ten80Ten's SMTP infrastructure to email arbitrary team_members. Use cases: (a) spam phishing emails 'from' Ten80Ten branded HTML to staff with attacker-controlled comment/captionPreview, (b) burn SMTP quota, (c) leak the list of @-mentioned team_members (the route returns `matched: members.map(m=>m.name)` which confirms who is on the team). The mention route's response body even includes matched names, an enumeration oracle (200 with `matched:[]` vs 200 with names).
findings.repro: curl -X POST https://smm.ten80ten.com/api/notifications/mention -H 'content-type: application/json' -d '{"comment":"@Aldridge Dagos urgent: click https://evil.com/login","postTitle":"Q2 Plan","postId":"<any>","authorName":"Aldridge","authorEmail":"x@y"}'  # 200 + sent>0; phishing email lands in Aldridge's inbox from hello@ten80ten.com with Ten80Ten branding
findings.fix: Add requireBearerTeamRole / requireBearerUser at the top of every notifications/* route. Derive authorName / approvedBy / requestedBy from the verified token's email (look up team_members), not the body. Remove `matched` names from the response.

findings.title: team/request-access endpoint lacks CAPTCHA — IP-only rate limit allows distributed spam of admin inboxes
findings.impact: 5 requests/IP/minute and zero CAPTCHA means a residential-proxy botnet can drive 1000+ signup_requests per minute, blasting Ten80Ten admins' inboxes (the route emails ALL superadmin+admin team members) and saturating signup_requests with chaff. Anti-enumeration is preserved (same shape response for duplicates), but the admin emailing path is not deduplicated across the network. SMTP TRANSPORTER.sendMail is called inside a loop with `to: adminEmails.join(", ")` — one outbound message per request × N admins.
findings.repro: Rotate through Tor / residential proxies, POST varied request bodies. Admin email volume scales linearly with attacker IP pool.
findings.fix: Add a CAPTCHA (Cloudflare Turnstile or hCaptcha) verified server-side. Also de-dupe outbound admin notifications by hashing (email+last_24h_bucket) and skip if already sent in window. Drop the admin emails join — send individually with BCC.

findings.title: logger logs full user email + names in workspace/provision and audit paths (PII in console)
findings.evidence: console.log(`[workspace/provision] Provisioned ${user.email} as ${role} (${status})`);
findings.impact: user.email + role go to Vercel logs (retention 30+ days depending on plan) and any log-shipper (Datadog, Logtail, etc.). For a team of ~10 this is low-volume but it accumulates PII unnecessarily. GDPR / CCPA scope. Same pattern in `[request-access]` (logs ${email}), invite (logs ${cleanEmail} indirectly via thrown error messages), and notifications/* failed email loops which log recipient addresses.
findings.repro: Provision any new user; tail Vercel function logs → see plaintext email + role.
findings.fix: Replace bare email logs with hashed-email or use the structured log helper at lib/logger.ts (which appears to scrub by default). Use `correlation_id` for traceability instead.

findings.title: auth/setup avatar upload path is predictable + permits overwrite of other users' avatars
findings.impact: Uses `upsert: true` keyed by email-derived path. If RLS on storage.objects.avatars allows authenticated INSERT/UPDATE on any path (default for many setups), a malicious user with their own session can craft a path like `competing_user_at_example_com-<time>.jpg` and overwrite a colleague's avatar. The path-component uses `user.email` from getUser (their own), but `upload(path, file)` does not validate the path against the actor — only Supabase storage RLS does. Worth verifying that storage policies actually scope by auth.uid() prefix.
findings.repro: Authenticated user calls `supabase.storage.from('avatars').upload('victim_at_company_com-1234.jpg', evilFile, {upsert:true})` directly from the browser console. If avatars bucket has the default 'authenticated can write' policy, it succeeds and replaces the victim's avatar.
findings.fix: Verify the avatars bucket has a Storage RLS policy that requires path LIKE `<auth.uid()>/*`. Update the upload path to use `${user.id}/${Date.now()}.${ext}` so the policy is enforceable. Drop email-derived paths everywhere.

findings.title: Cookies cleared on logout but never set HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite explicitly
findings.impact: The logout route DELETES the cookies but I found no code path that ever SETS sb-access-token / sb-refresh-token as HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Lax cookies on the server. The Supabase client SDK persists tokens in localStorage by default (persistSession defaults to true for browser clients) — these are XSS-readable. Combined with the long refresh-token TTL, an XSS bug anywhere in the app would let attackers exfiltrate the tokens and replay them. The intended remediation (server-set cookies) is half-built — server reads them via createServerSupabaseClient (line 32 of supabase/server.ts) but no route actually sets them.
findings.repro: After login, inspect localStorage — you'll see sb-* keys with the access+refresh tokens. Document.cookie won't contain them. An XSS payload `document.body.innerHTML += ''` exfiltrates the session.
findings.fix: Add a POST /api/auth/login route that exchanges email+password for tokens server-side, then sets sb-access-token + sb-refresh-token as HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite=Lax cookies. Migrate client supabase init to use { auth: { storage: cookieStorage } } pattern via @supabase/ssr.

findings.title: publish-jobs accepts user-supplied Authorization header verbatim — anon-key wrapper rather than verified server-side
findings.impact: createCallerClient forwards the raw Authorization header into the Supabase client. caller.auth.getUser() validates the token via Supabase's auth endpoint, which IS correct. But the same Authorization header is then attached to ALL queries on `caller.*` — meaning if the user's token includes elevated claims (e.g. someone manipulated user_metadata.role at signup-page), they would inherit those claims into queries. Practical impact is low because Supabase JWTs are signed and not user-modifiable, but the pattern of `headers: authorization ? ... :{}` allows the caller to ALSO pass `apikey: <service-role>` if they figure out a way to inject. Better to construct a fresh client with just the cookie/Bearer token, not the full header dict.
findings.repro: Send Authorization: Bearer <valid-token>\r\napikey: <attacker-supplied-key> — depending on Next.js header handling, may or may not split. Low-likelihood but defense-in-depth gap.
findings.fix: Use createServerSupabaseClient (already in lib/supabase/server.ts) which reads only the cookie, never forwards the raw header. Or strip everything except Bearer before forwarding.

findings.title: team/remove-member doesn't verify member belongs to caller's workspace before delete
findings.evidence:
// ─── Delete from team_members ───
await admin.from("team_members").delete().eq("id", body.memberId);
findings.impact: remove-member only requires the caller to have role superadmin/admin/owner — but doesn't check that the targeted memberId is actually in the caller's workspace. In single-tenant deployment this is a non-issue. The moment multi-tenant lands, admin of workspace A can delete any team_member globally by passing their id. Same applies to the auth.admin.deleteUser call which removes the user from auth entirely.
findings.repro: Multi-tenant: admin of workspace A POSTs {memberId: '<id-of-member-in-workspace-B>', memberEmail:'victim@b.com'} → 200 success, member deleted cross-workspace.
findings.fix: Before delete, SELECT team_members.workspace_id WHERE id=memberId and assert it matches the actor's workspace_id (or that the actor is a superadmin of that workspace). Use the same pattern in resend-invite, approve-request, etc.

findings.title: notifications/mention regex parses @mentions naively — false positives email random team members
findings.impact: An attacker (per SEC-012, the route is unauthed) can mention a team member's full name even if not formatted as @ — but more subtly, the regex matches @-prefixed casual text inside any comment. Comment 'Could @ Sarah review this' would email Sarah whether or not the writer intended it. Combined with the unauth issue, this is a UX-grade phishing handle. With auth in place, this is just a low-severity false-positive bug.
findings.repro: Authenticated user posts comment 'I @ Bob whenever I want to email him' → Bob gets a 'You were mentioned' email even though no real mention was intended.
findings.fix: Require the mention to be preceded by whitespace or start-of-string (`(?<=^|\s)@`) and limit to capitalized words. Also dedupe by name and confirm against a whitelist of team_members.name strings derived server-side, not pattern-extracted.

findings.title: team_members lookup uses ilike() — leaks via case-collation across multi-tenant emails (future)
findings.evidence:
const { data } = await admin
  .from("team_members")
  .select("role")
  .ilike("email", email)
  .maybeSingle();
findings.impact: ilike for email lookup is correct for single-tenant case-insensitive matching, but if multi-tenant is added with email uniqueness scoped by workspace (i.e., same email allowed in two workspaces), .ilike + maybeSingle without workspace_id will silently return the first match — granting cross-tenant role inheritance. Already partially anchored to `BASELINE_WORKSPACE` in studio/access resolveWorkspace, but the requireBearerTeamRole helper itself has no workspace context.
findings.repro: Multi-tenant: user@gmail.com is editor in workspace A and viewer in workspace B. requireBearerTeamRole returns whichever ilike picks (no ORDER BY). Role check could permit elevated action in wrong workspace.
findings.fix: Refactor requireBearerTeamRole to accept an optional workspaceId and filter by it. Always pair email + workspace_id for membership lookups.

findings.title: auto-revise/process can be invoked via GET (alias for POST) — leaks worker queue size as URL-cacheable
findings.impact: GET requests with sensitive side effects (job processing, OpenAI calls) are an HTTP anti-pattern — CDN/proxy/log layers may cache the response (which includes processed:N), enabling URL-scrape attacks via referrer leaks. With CRON_SECRET as a query param (unlikely but possible) the secret could leak to Vercel access logs.
findings.repro: Crawler indexes a referrer header containing the secret if it ever leaks in URL. Multiple GET hits can cause duplicate job processing if the queue race window opens.
findings.fix: Remove the GET alias and configure Vercel cron to POST. If unavoidable, keep GET but `return NextResponse.redirect('/api/ai/auto-revise/process', 308)` so the secret stays in headers.

findings.title: Reset-password page only checks length≥8 — no rotation/complexity check against new=old
findings.evidence: const isValid = password.length >= 8 && password === confirm;
findings.impact: Server-side Supabase auth.updateUser may or may not enforce the 'different-from-old' rule depending on project config. Users can 'reset' their password to the same value, defeating the purpose of a reset triggered after suspected compromise. Also no breach-list check (HIBP).
findings.repro: Trigger reset; submit current password as new. Supabase by default accepts; password unchanged, attacker who phished the reset link can also use the same flow.
findings.fix: Enable Supabase auth.password_min_length AND require the rotated password be different from the old via a server-side admin.compare. Add HIBP k-anon check before accepting.

findings.title: drive/upload temporary safeName regex allows path traversal characters in extension
findings.evidence:
const safeName = body.fileName.replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9._-]/g, "_").replace(/_+/g, "_");
const driveFileName = `${dateStr}_${safeName}`;
findings.impact: Replaces forbidden chars but leaves `.` so a filename like `....jpg` or `..-secret.txt` is preserved as `....jpg` / `.._secret.txt`. Drive treats these literally so there's no real traversal, but the persisted filename is visible in audit metadata and admin UI. A more weird filename like `__proto__.js` could confuse downstream JSON manipulation if filename is ever used as an object key.
findings.repro: Upload with fileName: `__proto__.js` → driveFileName becomes `2026-05-20_..._proto__.js` and is stored verbatim. Cosmetic only.
findings.fix: Strip leading dots; cap length; lowercase. e.g., `safeName.replace(/^\.+/, '').slice(0,128)`.

findings.title: service-worker registration via dangerouslySetInnerHTML — strict-CSP-incompatible
findings.evidence:
<script
  dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
    __html: `if("serviceWorker"in navigator){navigator.serviceWorker.register("/sw.js")}`,
  }}
/>
findings.impact: Inline <script> blocks the ability to ship a strict Content-Security-Policy (script-src 'self' without 'unsafe-inline'). This is a hardening gap rather than a direct vuln — it means any future XSS would be unmitigated by CSP. Combined with SEC-016 (XSS-readable tokens in localStorage), CSP is the only defense-in-depth left.
findings.repro: Try adding `Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self'` to next.config response headers — page breaks because the inline SW registration script is blocked.
findings.fix: Move the SW registration into a separate /public/sw-register.js and load it with <script src=/sw-register.js defer/>. Then add strict CSP via next.config headers.
